annotations.label: Entropy (thermodynamic state variable)

anchor.snippet: Entropy is a thermodynamic state variable that quantifies the probabilistic distribution of accessible microstates in a system.

annotations.note: Thermodynamic entropy as a physical state variable is not formalized in Mathlib; only information-theoretic and topological-dynamical entropies (e.g. `Real.negMulLog`, `coverEntropy`) exist.

annotations.label: Second law of thermodynamics (entropy formulation)

anchor.snippet: the entropy of an isolated system left to spontaneous evolution cannot decrease with time

annotations.note: No thermodynamic framework exists in Mathlib, so the second law has no formalization.

annotations.label: Clausius definition of entropy

anchor.snippet: defined it as the quotient of an infinitesimal amount of heat to the instantaneous temperature

annotations.note: Clausius' dS = δQ/T definition has no thermodynamic counterpart in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Boltzmann statistical entropy

anchor.snippet: explained entropy as the measure of the number of possible microscopic arrangements or states of individual atoms and molecules of a system that comply with the macroscopic condition of the system

annotations.note: Statistical-mechanics entropy concept is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Boltzmann's logarithmic definition of entropy

anchor.snippet: defined entropy as proportional to the natural logarithm of the number of microstates such a gas could occupy

annotations.note: S = k log W is the uniform-distribution special case of Shannon entropy, whose building block `Real.negMulLog` is in Mathlib; the physics constant and microstate counting are not.

annotations.label: State variables and state functions

anchor.section: State variables and functions of state
anchor.snippet: State variables depend only on the equilibrium condition, not on the path evolution to that state.

annotations.note: No thermodynamic state-space machinery in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Line integral of state function over reversible cycle is zero

anchor.section: State variables and functions of state
anchor.snippet: the change or line integral of any state function, such as entropy, over this reversible cycle is zero

annotations.note: While Mathlib has line integrals of exact differentials, the thermodynamic interpretation is not present.

annotations.label: Entropy change in a reversible process

anchor.snippet: The entropy change [MATH] of a system can be well-defined as a small portion of heat

annotations.note: Reversible-process entropy change is a thermodynamic notion absent from Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: The entropy change [MATH] of a system can be well-defined as a small portion of heat

annotations.label: Irreversible processes increase total entropy

anchor.snippet: an irreversible process increases the total entropy of the system and surroundings

annotations.note: Thermodynamic irreversibility is not formalized.

annotations.label: Carnot cycle heat transfers

anchor.snippet: In a Carnot cycle, the heat [MATH] is transferred from a hot reservoir to a working gas at the constant temperature

annotations.note: Carnot cycle has no formalization in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: In a Carnot cycle, the heat [MATH] is transferred from a hot reservoir to a working gas at the constant temperature

annotations.label: Carnot's theorem

anchor.snippet: a heat engine with two thermal reservoirs can produce a work [MATH] if and only if there is a temperature difference between reservoirs

annotations.note: Carnot's theorem on heat engines is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fundamental postulate of statistical mechanics

anchor.snippet: among system microstates of the same energy (i.e., degenerate microstates ) each microstate is assumed to be populated with equal probability

annotations.note: Statistical-mechanics postulates are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.note: Ensembles of statistical mechanics are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Second law of thermodynamics (general statement)

anchor.snippet: The second law of thermodynamics requires that, in general, the total entropy of any system does not decrease other than by increasing the entropy of some other system.

annotations.note: Second law is a physical principle not present in Mathlib.

annotations.note: Lieb–Yngvason axiomatic entropy is not formalized.

annotations.label: Shannon entropy

anchor.snippet: The definition of information entropy is expressed in terms of a discrete set of probabilities

annotations.note: Mathlib has the summand `Real.negMulLog` and the q-ary/binary cases `Real.qaryEntropy`, `Real.binEntropy`, but not the general Shannon entropy `H(X) = Σ negMulLog pᵢ` over a distribution.

annotations.label: Bekenstein–Hawking black hole entropy

anchor.snippet: The entropy of a black hole is proportional to the surface area of the black hole's event horizon

annotations.note: Bekenstein–Hawking entropy S = kA/(4ℓ_P²) is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Black holes maximise entropy

anchor.snippet: Jacob Bekenstein and Stephen Hawking have shown that black holes have the maximum possible entropy of any object of equal size.

annotations.note: Maximality of black-hole entropy is not formalized.
